name: The Beelzebul controversy
notes: Matthew, Mark, and Luke each record the charge that Jesus casts out devils by Beelzebub the prince of the devils, and His reply. The shared core runs through the same beats: the accusation, a kingdom or house divided against itself, Satan divided against himself, the strong man bound and his goods spoiled, and the warning of blasphemy against the Holy Ghost. Matthew and Luke open with the healing of the possessed dumb man and add the rebuttals 'by whom do your children cast them out' and 'if I by the Spirit/finger of God cast out devils, then the kingdom of God is come', along with 'he that is not with me is against me'. Mark gives the briefest frame but uniquely grounds the unpardonable sin in their saying 'He hath an unclean spirit'. Luke's parallel to the Holy Ghost saying sits later at 12:10 and is left for further study, so the blasphemy warning is linked here only between Matthew and Mark. Where each Gospel keeps its own emphasis (Luke's armed strong man keeping his palace, Matthew's son of David, Mark's scribes from Jerusalem) the accounts stand as complementary vantages on one occasion.
